title: EU closes Article 50 consultation as August AI Act deadline approaches
subtitle: The European Commission's public consultation on AI-generated content labeling and watermarking ended June 3, with binding rules taking effect August 2, 2026.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: The European Commission closed its public consultation on draft transparency guidelines for the EU AI Act on June 3, 2026. The consultation covered Article 50 — the section that requires providers of generative AI to inform users when they are interacting with an AI system and to embed machine-readable marks in synthetic content so it can be detected as AI-generated or manipulated. The binding obligations under Article 50 become applicable on August 2, 2026, less than two months out.

The closing of the consultation matters because the Commission is now finalizing two parallel documents: the legally binding guidelines on Article 50 and a voluntary Code of Practice on marking and labelling AI-generated content. A second draft of the Code was published in March; the final version is expected before the August deadline. Companies operating in the EU need to know which obligations are mandatory and which are voluntary safe harbors, and that line gets drawn in the final text.

The deadline pressure is real. Article 50 affects every U.S. company that ships a generative AI product to EU users — image generators, chatbots, voice clones, video tools — not just EU-based providers. Watermarking requirements alone have been technically contested for two years, with research repeatedly showing that imperceptible watermarks can be stripped by light post-processing. The Commission has acknowledged the technical limits but is moving forward on the schedule anyway, leaning on the voluntary Code to fill in the gaps.

A note for learners: if you build or ship anything that creates synthetic content, this regulation will reach you whether or not you live in Europe. The practical move is to learn what 'machine-readable provenance' actually means in code — C2PA content credentials, SynthID-style watermarks, signed metadata — because compliance teams are about to be asking. Knowing the technical primitives now puts you ahead of the August scramble.

sources.title: Consultation on the draft guidelines on transparency obligations under the AI Act — European Commission
Code of Practice on marking and labelling of AI-generated content — European Commission
The EU AI Act's Transparency Rules: A Practical Guide to Article 50 — artificialintelligenceact.eu
